channel.title: World news | The Guardian
channel.description: Latest World news news, comment and analysis from the Guardian, the world's leading liberal voice
channel.copyright: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026
channel.dc:rights: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026

item.title: Two Russian men jailed in Angola for terrorism and spying
item.description:
The men were convicted over alleged plan to stage coup and organise deadly demonstrations over fuel prices last year

Two Russian men have been found guilty by an Angolan court of terrorism and spying after being accused of planning a coup and helping to organise deadly demonstrations in the southern African country last year.

Lev Lakshtanov and Igor Ratchin were sentenced to eight and 11 years in jail respectively. They had denied the charges, claiming they were in Angola for cultural and business activities.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Ministerio do Interior/Governo de Angola
Photograph: Ministerio do Interior/Governo de Angola
Photograph: Ministerio do Interior/Governo de Angola

item.dc:creator: Rachel Savage in Johannesburg and agencies

item.title: MoD cancels army training in Kenya over powers to prosecute British soldiers
item.description:
September exercise called off after Kenya raises concerns over funding and jurisdiction for criminal offences

The British army has been forced to cancel training for its troops in Kenya after the Kenyan government refused to ratify a bilateral defence agreement.

While the UK government wanted to renew the existing agreement, Kenyan lawmakers had sought changes to give local authorities greater powers to hold British soldiers accountable for any offences committed in the country.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Al-Othman North of England correspondent

item.title: Health worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC
item.description:
UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease

A humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital in London after potential exposure to the Ebola virus.

The UK resident was medically evacuated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Nadeem Badshah

item.title: Healthy diet too expensive for one in three people globally, UN report finds
item.description:
Global hunger declined for the third year in a row in 2025 but experts call for action to bring down costs of fruit, vegetables and dairy as report shows 2.69bn people can’t afford to eat well

One in three people cannot afford to eat a healthy diet, a new report has found, prompting calls for urgent action on food affordability.

There are 2.69 billion people unable to afford a diverse diet that meets their energy and nutrient needs, and it will take “immense” amounts of work to achieve the global target of zero hunger by 2030, the annual report from five UN agencies warned.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Kat Lay Global health correspondent

item.title: São Tomé and Príncipe heads to polls in tense presidential election
item.description:
Incumbent Carlos Vila Nova hopes to defeat his former party and secure second term as independent

Voters in São Tomé and Príncipe go to the polls for a presidential election on Sunday as one of Africa’s least populous countries seeks to burnish its democratic credentials.

According to the National Election Commission, about 142,000 people are registered to vote in the tiny African state’s elections, approximately 15% of whom live in the diaspora.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Eromo Egbejule in Abidjan

item.title: Guyanese authorities confirm death of 72 people in sinking of coastal ferry
item.description:
Of the estimated 179 people aboard, 76 were rescued as efforts continue to salvage the ship and recover all bodies

The confirmed death toll from the recent capsizing of a state ferry off Guyana’s coast has risen to 72, with more bodies recovered as a multinational team struggle to salvage the partially submerged vessel.

Of the estimated 179 people aboard, 76 people were rescued. The government has estimated that 100 may have died in the accident, which would make it the worst marine tragedy on record for the South American nation.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Associated Press in Georgetown

item.title: Canadian government urged to block Thomson Reuters data deal with US ICE
item.description:
US agency is to pay Canadian media company US$125m for use of investigative database to detect ‘immigration fraud’

A Canadian federal opposition party leader has called on Mark Carney’s government to block the latest data-sharing deal between US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and the Canadian media company Thomson Reuters.

Avi Lewis, the federal leader of the New Democratic party (NDP), demanded on Tuesday that Ottawa address the contract and further restrict Canadian corporations from doing business with ICE, after Canada’s National Observer news website reported on the deal.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Olivia Bowden in Toronto

item.title: Masked men invading and taking Amazon communities ‘hostage’ in organised crime surge, say local leaders
item.description:
Delegation sent to Brasília after gunmen from organised crime group enter homes in western Amazon

Indigenous leaders on the Brazilian-Peruvian border have said their communities are being “taken hostage” by organised crime groups who are running rampant across the region, destroying rainforest, building roads and trafficking drugs.

Earlier this month, masked men armed with semi-automatic weapons entered homes in Apiwtxa, a village in the Kampa do Rio Amônia Indigenous territory, intimidating people and searching for targets.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Watts

item.title: Weather tracker: More than 900 wildfires rage across Canada
item.description:
Fires remain out of control in Northwest Territories and Ontario, while Argentina endures record night-time heat

Recent hot, dry weather in Canada has triggered hundreds of wildfires across the country. As of Wednesday, more than 900 were burning, scorching about 3m hectares of land. While fires have broken out in several provinces, the greatest concern is in the Northwest Territories and Ontario, where several remain out of control.

Poor air quality has been reported across affected regions, with wildfire smoke carried by winds into the US and even across the Atlantic to northern Europe. The risk of further blazes remains high as North America enters the peak of fire season.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: James Michelin for MetDesk

item.title: Mayor shot dead in Mexico months after surviving assassination attempt
item.description:
Valentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024

A mayor was shot dead on Wednesday in the Mexican state of Morelos after having survived a previous assassination attempt earlier this year. He is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in October 2024.

Valentín Lavín Romero, who was from the Green party, an ally of the ruling leftwing Morena party, was wounded at the end of January when hitmen shot him at a petrol station on a motorway.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Thomas Graham

item.title: Total solar eclipse books out hotels two years in advance in New Zealand city
item.description:
Dunedin will be the focal point, having last experienced such an eclipse in 1163 – before any humans reached New Zealand

Hotels are booking out two years in advance in the New Zealand city of Dunedin as it prepares for the region’s first total solar eclipse in more than 850 years.

At 4.17pm on 22 July 2028, the moon will pass in front of the sun, casting a roughly 100km-wide shadowy band across parts of southern New Zealand. Everywhere within that band – from tourist hotspot Queenstown in the west to Dunedin city in the east – will be plunged into daytime darkness for 2 minutes and 51 seconds.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Eva Corlett in Wellington

item.title: British woman jailed in Hong Kong for blackmail after accusing banker of rape
item.description:
Isabel Rose, 26, sentenced to six years for blackmail and perverting course of public justice over her allegations against British man

A British woman who reported being raped to police in Hong Kong has been sentenced to six years in prison for blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.

Isabel Rose, 26, from south-east London, travelled to Hong Kong in early 2024 to visit a British banker she had met while travelling in south-east Asia.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Danny Vincent

item.title: Australia to warn China it won’t be bullied by ‘provocative‘ actions as it builds military and nuclear arsenal
item.description:
Foreign minister Penny Wong will deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Penny Wong will warn China that Australia and other neighbouring nations will not be bullied by its “provocative” actions, after its involvement in another “destabilising and dangerous” incident this week.

The foreign affairs minister – who will deliver the address on Thursday in the Philippines, where the 59th Asean foreign minister’s meeting is being held – will also caution world leaders against placing a toll on the strait of Hormuz after both Iran and the US threatened to do amid the protracted conflict.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sarah Basford Canales and Luca Ittimani

item.title: Michelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea faces jail over ant dessert
item.description:
Prosecutors seek a one-year sentence and heavy fine after chef garnished dish with insects not approved for consumption

Prosecutors in South Korea have asked a court to sentence the owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant to one year in prison for serving a dessert topped with ants, an insect not approved for consumption under local food safety law.

The request, made on Monday, also sought a 20m won (£10,108) fine for the company that runs the restaurant, Yonhap news agency reported.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea

item.dc:creator: Raphael Rashid

item.title: Japan records its first ‘cruelly hot day’ as cities swelter in 40-degree heat
item.description:
In April Japan coined new term kokushobi for days above 40C as country advises workers to wear shorts to the office to cope with stifling heat

Japan logged its first official kokushobi or “cruelly hot day” on Tuesday since the new term for when temperatures top 40 degrees Celsius was introduced in April. The Japan Meteorological Agency coined the new designation to warn the public of the heat dangers of days that were once freak weather events but have now become more common.

The central Japan cities of Gujo and Tajimi in Gifu prefecture, along with Toyota in Aichi prefecture, all hit 40C. Gujo’s 40C and Toyota’s 40.1C were all-time highs recorded at their respective weather stations.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Gavin Blair in Tokyo

item.title: Australian households face prospect of interest rate hike and petrol prices rising above $2 a litre
item.description:
Economists’ warning comes as escalating Middle East crisis pushes global crude oil back above $US100 a barrel

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Australian households face the prospect of a Reserve Bank interest rate hike and petrol prices above $2 a litre over the coming weeks, economists warn, as the escalating Middle East crisis pushes global crude back above $US100 a barrel.

Financial markets now see an even chance the RBA board will deliver a fourth cash rate increase at the next meeting on 11 August.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Patrick Commins Economics editor

item.title: Pauline Hanson’s domestic violence comments were no stumble
item.description:
The One Nation leader has allied herself with men’s rights activists for years, consistently suggesting women routinely make false allegations of violence

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

For a decade, Pauline Hanson has been beating the same drum.

Her comments this week – suggesting domestic violence was a “two-way street” and questioning why women did not simply leave abusive relationships – have caused significant concern and been labelled “dangerous” by experts and other commentators.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Ben Smee

item.title: University courses covering Israel-Palestine should be audited, Julian Leeser tells royal commission
item.description:
Inquiry’s focus this week is on security for the Jewish community, with the MP telling the commissioner his son is afraid to attend synagogues and events

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

The shadow education minister, Julian Leeser, has called for universities to undertake an audit of any classes that teach about the Jewish community, Israel or Palestine.

Appearing before the royal commission into antisemitism and social cohesion on Friday, Leeser said he had been “very concerned” about antisemitism on university campuses and was not satisfied with how vice-chancellors had addressed the issue after the 7 October 2023 Hamas terrorist attack.

Continue reading...
item.category: Royal commission on antisemitism and social cohesion
item.dc:creator: Caitlin Cassidy

item.title: Julian Leeser says his son no longer feels safe at Jewish events – as it happened
item.description:
This blog is now closed

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Australian exports sent to the US will be hit with a 12.5% tariff as the US president imposes levies on more than 80 nations.

The Trump administration unveiled the latest tranche of tariffs for countries over their “failure to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labour”.

This, in our view, makes no sense.

Forced labour is definitely something which needs to be combated around the world, so we support that objective.

We absolutely need to see the strait of Hormuz be open and for freedom of navigation to happen there, for commercial shipping to be allowed through, and we very much call on Iran to allow that to be the case.

We need to see a restoration to the normal global fuel supply chain because it’s having a significant impact around the world.

Continue reading...
item.category: Royal commission on antisemitism and social cohesion
item.dc:creator: Achol Arok (now) and Catie McLeod and Kat Wong (earlier)

item.title: Donald Trump hits Australian exports to US with new higher trade tariff over claims of ‘forced labour’
item.description:
Australia’s trade minister, Don Farrell, says tariff regime is unjustified and inconsistent with free trade agreement with US

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Donald Trump has hit Australian exports with new trade tariffs, claiming the country is not doing enough to combat forced labour, but the Albanese government has demanded he remove them immediately.

The United States announced on Friday morning (AEST) that many Australian exports now attract a new 12.5% tariff as part of a broader regime affecting dozens of countries. However the Albanese government says Australia’s three largest export categories – including beef and gold – will avoid the tariff hit.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Josh Butler and Sarah Basford Canales

item.title: More than 80,000 people flee as wildfires spread in south-west France and Spain
item.description:
‘Unpredictable’ blaze forces evacuation of holiday spot Cap Ferret and Spain declares emergency over fires near Madrid

More than 80,000 people have fled huge wildfires in Spain and France as Madrid declared a national emergency and French authorities ordered the total evacuation, by land and sea, of one of the country’s best-known holiday spots.

Authorities in south-west France ordered the Cap Ferret peninsula on the Atlantic coast to be evacuated “village by village” in the face of an “intense, highly unpredictable” wildfire that has burned through more than 12,500 hectares (30,900 acres) of forest.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jon Henley in Paris and Sam Jones in Madrid

item.title: France evacuates Cap Ferret peninsula as Spanish wildfires merge near Madrid – as it happened
item.description:
French evacuees using hundreds of mobilised boats or the only road linking the area with the mainland as evacuation also ordered in areas near Spanish capital

Over in France, it’s not just the south-west that gets affected, but the country’s south-east regions are also being hard hit, AFP noted.

Dozens of fires flared up and are being fanned by the mistral wind around the historic village of Cotignac – officials saying around 25 houses had been destroyed since Tuesday.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jakub Krupa (now) and Tom Ambrose (earlier)

item.title: Burnham urged to lobby EU leaders directly to waive EES border controls
item.description:
Lib Dems call on PM to push for suspension of ‘bungled, malfunctioning’ system as overseas holiday travel peaks

Andy Burnham has been urged to lobby EU leaders directly to suspend the new entry-exit system, EES, as the peak weekend for overseas holidays begins.

About 2.2 million British holidaymakers are expected to head abroad between Friday and Monday, the majority to Europe, with all schools in the UK now on their summer break.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Gwyn Topham Transport correspondent

item.title: How dangerous are wildfires and how can we stop them from getting worse?
item.description:
The conditions for ferocious fires are expected to worsen as the planet heats up, putting more people at risk

Fierce wildfires after back-to-back heatwaves have forced tens of thousands of people across southern Europe to flee their homes, while powerful infernos across Canada and the US choke big cities with thick smoke.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Ajit Niranjan

item.title: Poland’s main rightwing opposition splits after loyalty pledge ultimatum
item.description:
Former prime minister Mateusz Morawiecki and allies say they were forced out over factional disagreements

Poland’s main opposition Law and Justice (PiS) party has split, after former prime minister Mateusz Morawiecki and his allies said they were forced out over factional disagreements on policy direction ahead of next year’s parliamentary election.

Speaking at a press conference, the party’s longtime leader, Jarosław Kaczyński, said dozens of members of parliament effectively left the party after refusing to sign a loyalty oath.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jakub Krupa

item.title: Four Palestinians and one Israeli killed during West Bank settler attack
item.description:
Several others seriously injured in attack that residents of Palestinian village say also involved Israeli military

Israel has said it is preparing a major military operation in the West Bank after four Palestinians and two Israelis were killed during a confrontation that began after armed settlers entered a Palestinian village with support from the Israeli military.

Several more people were seriously injured at the scene after a scuffle broke out over an Israeli settler’s weapon and the military opened fire, according to residents. Dozens more have been reported wounded in ensuing settler attacks on neighbouring Palestinian towns.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Andrew Roth, Sufian Taha, Quique Kierszenbaum and agencies in Jerusalem

item.title: Middle East crisis latest: US carries out strikes across Iran as Tehran targets American bases in region
item.description:
Latest attacks come after Trump vowed ‘major military punishment’ for Tehran and its Houthi allies

Donald Trump said that in recent meetings with Xi Jinping and Vladimir Putin, both leaders assured the US president separately that they would not sell weapons to Iran.

“Considering our relationship, I take him at his word,” Trump said of the Chinese leader. Meanwhile, the US president said that the Kremlin made the same guarantees, despite the ongoing war in Ukraine. Putin understands that the US does not sell weapons to Ukraine, “but to Nato countries”, Trump said in a post on Truth Social on Friday. “They pay full price, and how those weapons are distributed, I have no idea.”

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Lucy Campbell (now) and Mabel Banfield-Nwachi (earlier)

item.title: Middle East crisis is ‘getting out of control’, UN chief warns – as it happened
item.description:
This live blog has not closed, you can read our latest story from the region here

Earlier in the US day, before Iran’s Revolutionary Guard said it had stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait of Hormuz, Donald Trump issued this threat on his Truth Social platform:

From this point forward, any time the Islamic Republic of Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, whether it be by Missile, Rocket, Drone, or any other device or weapon, the United States will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT, including those located next to, or in, the Capital City of Tehran.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Yerushalmy, Lucy Campbell, Maya Yang, Vivian Ho, Taz Ali, Siraj Datoo and Adam Fulton

item.title: UK ‘ready to defend itself’ after Iranian threat over US bases
item.description:
IRGC claims base in south-west England was used by US to launch bombing missions, making it ‘legitimate target’

The government has said the UK is “ready to defend itself” after Iran’s military warned that any bases being used by the US were “legitimate targets”.

The UK has allowed the US to launch “defensive” operations from British bases hosting American planes since the start of its war on Iran but has refused to help in offensive operations.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hayden Vernon

item.title: Donald Trump says decision on ‘massive attack’ against Iran is close
item.description:
President’s remarks follow Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers in the Red Sea and oil prices rising past $100 a barrel again

Donald Trump has said he is close to deciding whether to launch “a massive attack” against Iran, on a scale larger than strikes already witnessed during the months-long conflict with Tehran.

Speaking to the Axios news site, the US president said: “I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before. I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it.”

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Peter Beaumont Senior international correspondent

item.title: Indian Cockroach activist ends hunger strike after 26 days as protests spread
item.description:
Sonam Wangchuk secures ‘hard bargain’ with Delhi but talks between government and movement leaders stall

The Cockroach activist Sonam Wangchuk has ended a 26-day hunger strike, as youth-led protests continued to erupt across India and leaders of the movement said there was no breakthrough in talks with the government.

Wangchuk had been fasting since June in solidarity with the Cockroach Janata party (CJP), a new youth-led movement that has been holding mass protests calling for government accountability and the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, over exam paper leaks. The leaks affected 2 million students in May and have been linked to more than a dozen suicides.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: The ‘cockroaches’ marching on India’s government – podcast
item.description:
South Asia correspondent Hannah Ellis-Petersen on the Cockroach movement – and the students driving India’s biggest protests in years

‘I’ve never seen this many young people out on the streets,’ says Hannah Ellis-Petersen.

For days, tens of thousands of Indians have been marching under the banner of the Cockroach Janta party.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Presented by Helen Pidd with Hannah Ellis-Petersen; produced by Saskia Collette, Alex Atack and Brian McNamara; executive producer Eli Block

item.title: ‘Cockroach’ protests: India’s Modi promises to punish officials over exam leaks
item.description:
PM says ‘those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared’ as scandal spirals into widespread anger

Narendra Modi has vowed to set up “fast-track courts” to speed up the punishment of officials accused of leaking examination papers, as the Indian prime minister seeks to quell a youth-led “cockroach” protest movement that has rocked Delhi and posed one of the biggest challenges to his government.

In his first public remarks on the movement, Modi posted on X: “Nothing is more important than the welfare and future of our youth … Those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared.”

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Amrit Dhillon in Delhi

item.title: Opposition parties shut down Indian parliament in solidarity with ‘cockroach’ protests
item.description:
Proceedings suspended as politicians call for Modi government to address police violence and education scandals

India’s opposition parties have shut down parliament in a protest calling for the resignation of the education minister, in solidarity with the new youth-led “cockroach” movement.

Opposition politicians, led by the Congress party leader Rahul Gandhi, gathered outside the parliament building in Delhi on Wednesday, many dressed in black and waving placards calling on Dharmendra Pradhan to quit.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: India’s Cockroach movement protesters show no sign of backing down
item.description:
Thousands of people again on the streets of Delhi calling for education reforms despite police violence on Monday

Taru had come to the protest prepared this time. In one hand was an anti-government poster and in the other was a bicycle helmet. “We need to have protection in case Delhi police beat us up again,” said the 25-year-old, who stood among her friends. “We are not scared of them and we won’t run away, no matter what they do. We’ll stay here till they listen.”

On Tuesday, central Delhi still thronged with thousands of protesters, as an unprecedented expression of dissent against the powerful ruling Narendra Modi government showed no sign of abating.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: Parts of England under heat alert with 30C temperatures possible
item.description:
Warning in place until Sunday morning in southern and central regions after drought declared in parts of Wales

Heat alerts are in place across parts of England, with possible highs of 30C (86F) and officials saying temperatures are likely to have an impact on health and wellbeing.

The yellow heat health alerts issued for the East Midlands, east of England, London, south-east, south-west and West Midlands are in place until 9am on Sunday.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hayden Vernon and agency

item.title: Reform councillors told to call donations inquiry ‘establishment stitch-up’
item.description:
Party briefing notes say: ‘Daily routine of hit jobs and smears will not deter us from winning next general election’

Reform UK has instructed its councillors to describe a police investigation into donations from the mother of George Cottrell as an establishment stitch-up, as polling showed the party’s popularity plummeting.

Internal briefing documents circulated to the party’s councillors included instructions to respond to questions about a Metropolitan police investigation into party donations with: “It is now clear that the establishment will try anything to stop us”, the Times revealed on Thursday.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Topping Political correspondent

item.title: Plus-size models and £5 tickets: Sinéad O’Dwyer’s runway show puts inclusivity first
item.description:
V&A event celebrates all shapes and sizes as wider fashion industry stalls on body diversity

Visitors to London’s Victoria and Albert Museum on Friday afternoon to watch Sinéad O’Dwyer’s Fashion in Motion show would have encountered something a little different. The cast of performers and models wore everything from underwear to skirt suits and sequins, and sometimes danced interpretively on the catwalk to a performance by the band Jeanie and The White Boys, but most notably came in all shapes and sizes.

O’Dwyer’s work has pushed against fashion’s insistence on a thin body type since she appeared at London fashion week in 2021. If most brands make the samples for clothes shown on the catwalk based on a size 4-8, she uses a size 18. She has won awards for her work, as well as celebrity fans including Cardi B and Björk.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Lauren Cochrane

item.title: From high heels to ‘Zara death pants’: how fashion falls foul of women’s safety
item.description:
A physio, a tailor and a historian on the style trends that can put women in hospital and how to avoid the dangers

“Buyer beware” is one of the oldest principles in English law. Now, a pair of wide legged trousers from Zara is bringing the phrase back to the forefront of shoppers’ minds.

On social media the £25.99 wide-legged trousers from the high-street retailer have earned the moniker “Zara death pants” thanks to a slew of videos showing wearers tripping over the leg of the trousers and injuring themselves.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Chloe Mac Donnell

item.title: Was Keir Starmer’s leaving party the boozy birth of a new Labour force?
item.description:
A £10,000 bar tab was drunk, there was a ‘lot of anger’, and now ex-ministers are thinking how to cooperate in future

Keir Starmer’s leaving party had been planned for some time. But in years to come it might be seen as the birth of a new Labour movement.

Dozens of ministers, aides and Starmer himself headed to the Grafton pub in Kentish Town on Monday night to drown their sorrows, hours after the former prime minister left Downing Street for the last time.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Kiran Stacey Policy editor

item.title: Trump’s new tariffs ‘a sales tax that will make life harder for Americans’, say Democrats – US politics live
item.description:
Even some Republicans apparently expressing concern over measures against 85 countries

  • What to know about Trump’s new tariffs on more than 80 countries

  • Sign up for the US Breaking News email

Represenatives Ilhan Omar and Pramila Jayapal, will visit the Whipple Federal Building in Minneapolis today to conduct a congressional oversight visit.

The Democratic lawmakers are visiting the site of sustained protests earlier this year against the immigration crackdown that swept the state of Minnesota, and resulted in the fatal shootings of two US citizens by federal immigration agents.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Marina Dunbar (now) Shrai Popat and Tom Ambrose (earlier)

item.title: Trump accused of using forced labor as ‘convenient’ justification for tariffs
item.description:
US president faced criticism from Democrats after tariffs of between 10% and 12.5% imposed on more than 80 countries

  • US politics live – latest updates

Donald Trump has been accused of using forced labor as “a pretext” to impose a fresh wave of tariffs on dozens of countries, as critics warned of higher prices for Americans.

The US president faced swift criticism from Democrats after his officials presented their latest effort to make his signature economic policy permanent following a string of high-profile legal setbacks. Some Republicans also expressed concern.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Gaya Gupta and Chris Stein in Washington

item.title: US cyclosporiasis outbreak expands to nine states as CDC investigates 7,400 cases
item.description:
Over 11,500 people this summer are suspected to have been sickened by disease that can cause explosive diarrhea, CDC says

Investigations into the largest cyclosporiasis outbreak on record expanded to nine states this week, as more than 11,500 people are suspected to have been sickened this summer, according to reports and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

Data released by the CDC this week said the agency is looking into 4,173 laboratory-confirmed domestic cases of the disease and investigating an additional 7,400.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jessica Glenza

item.title: Confusing product recall adds to US cyclospora outbreak uncertainty
item.description:
Explosive diarrhea infection detected in 41 states with at least 72 sickened by new, as yet unknown source of parasite

Confusion abounds in the cyclospora outbreak that has sickened thousands in at least 41 states, as officials identify a new cluster of cases and more potential products come under scrutiny.

The recall of products during this outbreak of explosive diarrhea has been an unusual one, complicated by poor communication, politicization and the delay of a new regulatory rule could have helped trace the cyclosporiasis outbreak faster.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Melody Schreiber

item.title: Justice Elena Kagan decries idea that US supreme court ‘rubber-stamps’ Trump’s agenda
item.description:
Kagan rejects notion that high court ‘does whatever the administration wants’, pointing to tariffs ruling and more

The liberal justice Elena Kagan has said it was “wrong” to accuse the US supreme court of becoming a rubber stamp for Donald Trump in his second term, amid criticism of its authorization of some of the president’s most controversial actions.

Pointing to rulings against the president on key issues such as tariffs, Kagan – one of three liberals on a court that has in recent years been defined by rulings authored by members of its six-justice conservative majority – defended it at an annual gathering of attorneys and judges for the ninth judicial circuit in Bellevue, Washington, on Thursday.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Chris Stein in Washington
